raw_case:
Rathke's cleft cyst apoplexy presented as pituitary apoplexy: A case report


Introduction and importance

Rathke's cleft cyst (RCC) are non-malignant lesions arising from remnants of Rathke's pouch in the pituitary gland. Apoplexy is seen commonly in pituitary macroadenomas but very rarely in RCCs.

Case presentation

A 30-year-old male presented with a severe headache and vomiting. Imaging revealed an enlarged pituitary gland with possible hemorrhage in a RCC. The cyst was evacuated with endonasal transsphenoidal surgery, and histology confirmed RCC. Follow-up imaging showed complete cyst resolution, although he required levothyroxine for hypothyroidism.

Clinical discussion

The occurrence of hemorrhage within RCCs, although rare, mimics pituitary tumor apoplexy often complicating the diagnosis. The management is similar to pituitary tumor apoplexy, primarily involving surgical intervention. Postoperative care may require long-term hormonal replacement therapy in a significant number of patients.

Conclusion

This report underscores the importance of considering RCC in the differential diagnoses of pituitary lesions with hemorrhage with promising surgical outcome.

Highlights

•Rathke's cleft cyst are non-malignant lesions arising from remnants of Rathke's pouch in the pituitary gland.•The rare occurrence of hemorrhage within RCCs mimics pituitary tumor apoplexy often complicating the diagnosis.•The management involves surgical intervention with postoperative hormonal dysfunction occurring rarely.1

Introduction

Rathke's cleft cyst (RCC) are benign cystic lesion within the sella turcica arising from epithelial remnants of Rathke's pouch [1,2]. RCC are mostly asymptomatic but sometimes they enlarge and can cause compressive effects on surrounding structures like pituitary gland and optic chiasma, making it symptomatic [1,3]. The common manifestations of symptomatic RCC are headaches, endocrinopathies, and visual impairment [1,3]. Very rarely, RCC can present with acute symptoms resembling pituitary apoplexy [1,[3], [4], [5]]. Apoplexy is seen in around 10.4 % of pituitary macroadenoma, 0.3 % in microadenoma, rarely in pineal tumors, and very rarely in RCC [6]. Here, we present a case of a 30-year-old male patient with RCC presenting as apoplexy.This work has been reported in line with the SCARE 2023 guideline [12].2

Case presentation

A 30-year-old male with no known comorbidities presented to the emergency department with a sudden onset of a headache that had progressively intensified over three days, accompanied by multiple episodes of non-projectile vomiting. There was no history of trauma, loss of consciousness, and abnormal body movements. He had no significant medical history, family history, or similar history in the past. On examination, the vital signs were within normal limits. A complete neurological examination including cranial nerves was unremarkable. The rest of the physical examination was unremarkable. Laboratory results revealed mild hyponatremia. A non-contrast computerized tomography (NCCT) of the head revealed an enlarged pituitary gland measuring about 13 mm (CC) × 13.2 mm (AP) × 16 mm (transverse). Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) showed a low T2 signal nodule measuring 5.6 × 9.5 mm within the gland. Fig. 1 On the post‑gadolinium images, there was peripheral rim enhancement of the gland with no enhancement in the majority of the central gland with effacement of the pituitary stalk. Fig. 2 These findings imply the potential occurrence of hemorrhage in a pituitary adenoma, Rathke cleft cyst (RCC), or craniopharyngioma. A surgical exploration was planned with transsphenoidal microsurgery which revealed a bulging of the sella floor. Upon opening the dura mater, there was an immediate expression of a bloody serous, mucinous, and yellowish substance. Cyst contents were completely evacuated and the cyst wall was partially excised. Fig. 3 On histological examination, the lesion was a sellar cyst with epithelial linings which was suggestive of RCC, as well as evidence of acute hemorrhage mixed in with the contents of the cyst. Fig. 4 Postoperatively, the patient's headaches improved. The patient developed secondary adrenal deficiency post operatively which resolved 2 months after the operation.At 3 months follow-up, the patient was asymptomatic and repeat imaging revealed complete resolution of the cyst. There were no changes in his hormonal assay except for an isolated increase in thyroid stimulating hormone (TSH value: 8 mU/L) and negative antithyroid peroxidase antibodies. His six weeks and 12 weeks follow-up showed no new changes.3

Clinical discussion

Rathke's cleft cysts (RCCs) are non-malignant lesions that originate from epithelial remnants of Rathke's pouch, a developmental structure in the pituitary gland [1]. RCCs primarily occur in the sellar and suprasellar regions of the brain and can range from a few millimeters to 1–2 cm [2,3]. These cysts demonstrate a higher prevalence within the age range of 30 to 50 years, with the mean age of presentation being 36.87 years, indicating a young mean age of presentation [2,7].RCC is a rare occurrence and has been sparsely documented in the scientific literature with a limited number of case reports [7]. RCC can rarely present with hemorrhage within the cyst, a distinct clinical manifestation that resembles the clinical syndrome of pituitary tumor apoplexy [8]. This unique presentation adds complexity to the differential diagnosis and requires careful evaluation to differentiate between the two conditions.Headache, specifically paroxysmal headache localized in the forehead, is the most frequent clinical manifestation observed in patients with symptomatic RCC [9] The other common clinical presentation of RCC apoplexy includes visual disturbances, hormonal dysfunction, and neurological deficits [1,3]. These symptoms arise due to the compression and impairment of the pituitary gland, optic chiasm, or adjacent brain structures by enlarged RCC [9,10]. Hyperprolactinemia is the most frequently observed disorder of the hypothalamic-pituitary axis, with hypogonadism, hypocortisolism, hypothyroidism, and diabetes insipidus following as other commonly affected conditions [7].The use of non-contrast computed tomography (NCCT) can be inconclusive to differentiate pituitary mass from RCC like in our case. The diagnosis of RCC apoplexy is typically made through neuroimaging techniques, such as magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), but according to a previous study, approximately 50 % of surgically confirmed RCCs were misdiagnosed initially as pituitary adenomas [11]. The clinical, radiographic, and surgical characteristics may contribute to the differential diagnosis of RCC apoplexy, but the confirmatory diagnosis of RCC apoplexy can be made only on histopathological confirmation [1,7]. RCC is histologically characterized by the presence of cuboidal or columnar epithelium containing goblet cells and cilia [1,2]. RCC with hemorrhage typically presents with distinct histological features, including the presence of hemosiderin pigment and infiltration of lymphocytes and plasma cells [7].The underlying cause of bleeding into an RCC remains unclear. Recent reports suggest that RCC apoplexy may be attributed to hemorrhage originating from compressed portal veins or newly formed delicate vessels within the granulation tissue of the cyst wall, particularly in larger cysts [1,7]. Since RCCs are lined by a fragile single layer of cuboidal or columnar epithelium including cilia and goblet cells, they are highly susceptible to rupture following minimal fluctuation in arterial pressure [1,2].Distinguishing RCCs from other sellar lesions can pose challenges in MRI imaging due to their similar appearances [7]. The signal intensities exhibited by an RCC on T1 and T2-weighted images can vary depending on its cystic content and the presence of an associated intracystic nodule [2,11]. Typically, this nodule presents as a region within the cyst that demonstrates T1-weighted hyperintensity, T2-weighted hypointensity and lacks gadolinium enhancement [1]. Furthermore, RCC and cystic pituitary adenoma can be differentiated by examining the presence of a fluid-fluid level, septation, off-midline location, and the existence of an intracystic nodule [11].The management approach for patients presenting with sudden bleeding into an RCC closely resembles that of individuals experiencing pituitary tumor apoplexy [1,7]. Surgical intervention is the established therapeutic approach for the treatment of RCC apoplexy [9]. Endoscopic endonasal transsphenoidal approach (EETA) has emerged as a standard treatment modality for symptomatic RCCs, offering potential benefits such as reduced surgical trauma and improved postoperative recovery rates [9]. The presence of epithelial squamous metaplasia in the histopathological examination can serve as a predictive factor for recurrence following surgical resection [7]. Postoperatively, a substantial proportion of patients, up to 24 %, necessitate long-term hormonal replacement therapy [7].4

Conclusion

This report underscores the importance of considering RCC in the differential diagnoses of pituitary lesions with hemorrhage with promising surgical outcome. Although rare, hemorrhage in RCC adds complexity to the diagnosis due to its resemblance to other pituitary lesions on MRI. Effective management relies on precise diagnosis with imaging, histopathology, and timely surgical intervention.

Consent

Written informed consent was obtained from the patient for publication of this case report and accompanying images. A copy of the written consent is available for review by the Editor-in-Chief of this journal on request.

Ethical approval

The case report is exempt from ethical approval in our institution.

Guarantor

Yugant Khand.

Research registration number

Not applicable.

Funding

This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors.

Provenance and peer review

Not commissioned, externally peer-reviewed.

Author contribution

All the authors contributed in conceptualizing, writing and editing the manuscript.

Conflict of interest statement

All authors declare that they have no conflict of interest.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information**: 30-year-old male with no known comorbidities.
- **Chief Complaint**: Severe headache and vomiting.
- **History of Present Illness**: Sudden onset of headache intensifying over three days, associated with multiple episodes of non-projectile vomiting. No history of trauma, loss of consciousness, or abnormal body movements.
- **Past Medical History**: None.
- **Personal History**: None.
- **Family History**: None.
- **Physical Examination**: Vital signs normal. Neurological and cranial nerve exams unremarkable. Physical examination otherwise unremarkable.
- **Ancillary Tests**: 
  - Laboratory: Mild hyponatremia.
  - Imaging: NCCT revealed an enlarged pituitary gland (13 × 13.2 × 16 mm). MRI showed a low T2 signal nodule (5.6 × 9.5 mm), peripheral rim enhancement, and central non-enhancement, suggesting potential hemorrhage within a pituitary lesion (possible adenoma, RCC, or craniopharyngioma).
  - Surgery: Transsphenoidal microsurgery revealed bloody, mucinous, and yellowish cystic contents. Histopathology confirmed RCC with epithelial lining and evidence of acute hemorrhage.
  - Follow-up: Complete resolution of the cyst on imaging; postoperative hypothyroidism requiring levothyroxine. Transient secondary adrenal insufficiency resolved within two months. No recurrence at the 3-month follow-up.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Potential Diagnoses Based on Imaging**:
   - Pituitary adenoma: Ruled out based on histopathology and absence of typical adenomatous features.
   - Rathke's cleft cyst (RCC): Considered due to imaging findings and confirmed on histopathology.
   - Craniopharyngioma: Excluded due to absence of characteristic calcifications and histological features.

2. **Surgical and Histopathological Analysis**:
   - Key findings included cystic contents with serous and yellowish fluid, epithelial linings, and acute hemorrhage, diagnostic of RCC hemorrhage.

3. **Exclusion of Other Lesions**:
   - MRI differentiation of RCC from other sellar lesions (e.g., adenomas or craniopharyngiomas) was challenging preoperatively. Confirmation relied on histological examination.

4. **Postoperative Evaluation**:
   - Persistent hypothyroidism and transient adrenal insufficiency were consistent with the compression and post-surgical manipulation of the pituitary gland.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: Rathke's cleft cyst with apoplexy (hemorrhage), mimicking pituitary apoplexy.

results.content:
### Resoning:
<step 1> **Analyze Chief Complaint and History of Present Illness:** The patient is a young male presenting with sudden onset of severe headache and vomiting that intensified over three days. This acute presentation, particularly the severity of the headache and associated vomiting, raises suspicion for intracranial pathology, such as hemorrhage, infection, or mass effect. The absence of trauma or loss of consciousness makes traumatic brain injury less likely, but doesn't exclude other intracranial issues.

<step 2> **Evaluate Physical and Neurological Examination:** The normal vital signs and unremarkable neurological and cranial nerve exams are initially reassuring. However, a normal neurological exam doesn't rule out intracranial pathology, especially in early or slowly progressing conditions.  It simply means there are no overt focal neurological deficits at the time of examination.  It's important to remember that subtle or early stage intracranial issues can present with generalized symptoms like headache before causing focal deficits.

<step 3> **Assess Ancillary Tests - Laboratory Findings:** Mild hyponatremia is noted. Hyponatremia in the context of headache and potential intracranial pathology is significant. It could be due to SIADH (Syndrome of Inappropriate Antidiuretic Hormone secretion), which can be associated with intracranial lesions, particularly pituitary lesions or conditions affecting the hypothalamus. This finding further strengthens the suspicion of intracranial involvement.

<step 4> **Interpret Ancillary Tests - Imaging Findings:** NCCT showing an enlarged pituitary gland is a crucial finding. This indicates a pituitary mass.  The size (13 × 13.2 × 16 mm) is significantly enlarged, suggesting a lesion occupying space within the sella turcica. The MRI further clarifies the nature of the lesion. The description of a low T2 signal nodule with peripheral rim enhancement and central non-enhancement strongly suggests hemorrhage within a pituitary lesion. The differential diagnoses provided by radiology (adenoma, RCC, craniopharyngioma) are all plausible pituitary/parasellar lesions that can undergo hemorrhage.  "RCC" here most likely refers to Rathke cleft cyst, a common benign cystic lesion in the pituitary region, rather than renal cell carcinoma.

<step 5> **Consider Surgical and Histopathological Findings:** Transsphenoidal microsurgery and the description of the surgical contents ("bloody, mucinous, and yellowish cystic contents") are highly suggestive of a Rathke cleft cyst. Rathke cleft cysts are typically cystic and can contain mucinous fluid and blood products, especially if hemorrhage has occurred.  The histopathology confirming "RCC with epithelial lining and evidence of acute hemorrhage" definitively diagnoses the lesion as a Rathke cleft cyst. The "epithelial lining" is characteristic of Rathke cleft cysts, which originate from remnants of Rathke's pouch.  "Acute hemorrhage" explains the patient's sudden onset of severe headache and vomiting, as hemorrhage into a Rathke cleft cyst can cause rapid cyst expansion and acute symptoms due to pressure on surrounding structures.

<step 6> **Review Follow-up Information:** Postoperative resolution of the cyst on imaging confirms successful surgical removal. Postoperative hypothyroidism and transient secondary adrenal insufficiency are recognized complications of transsphenoidal surgery due to manipulation of the pituitary gland. These are manageable with hormone replacement and are expected to resolve or stabilize over time. The absence of recurrence at 3-month follow-up is a positive indicator of successful treatment.

<step 7> **Synthesize all findings to reach the Final Diagnosis:**  Combining the clinical presentation of sudden severe headache and vomiting, imaging findings of a hemorrhagic pituitary lesion, surgical findings of cystic contents, and definitive histopathological diagnosis of "RCC with epithelial lining and evidence of acute hemorrhage," the final diagnosis is a Rathke cleft cyst with acute hemorrhage. The term "RCC" in the histopathology report is clearly an abbreviation for Rathke cleft cyst in this neurosurgical context.

### Answer:
Rathke cleft cyst with acute hemorrhage

This acute presentation, particularly the severity of the headache and associated vomiting, raises suspicion for intracranial pathology, such as hemorrhage, infection, or mass effect. The absence of trauma or loss of consciousness makes traumatic brain injury less likely, but doesn't exclude other intracranial issues.

**Evaluate Physical and Neurological Examination:** The normal vital signs and unremarkable neurological and cranial nerve exams are initially reassuring. However, a normal neurological exam doesn't rule out intracranial pathology, especially in early or slowly progressing conditions.  It simply means there are no overt focal neurological deficits at the time of examination.  It's important to remember that subtle or early stage intracranial issues can present with generalized symptoms like headache before causing focal deficits.

**Assess Ancillary Tests - Laboratory Findings:** Mild hyponatremia is noted. Hyponatremia in the context of headache and potential intracranial pathology is significant. It could be due to SIADH (Syndrome of Inappropriate Antidiuretic Hormone secretion), which can be associated with intracranial lesions, particularly pituitary lesions or conditions affecting the hypothalamus. This finding further strengthens the suspicion of intracranial involvement.

**Interpret Ancillary Tests - Imaging Findings:** NCCT showing an enlarged pituitary gland is a crucial finding. This indicates a pituitary mass.  The size (13 × 13.2 × 16 mm) is significantly enlarged, suggesting a lesion occupying space within the sella turcica. The MRI further clarifies the nature of the lesion. The description of a low T2 signal nodule with peripheral rim enhancement and central non-enhancement strongly suggests hemorrhage within a pituitary lesion. The differential diagnoses provided by radiology (adenoma, RCC, craniopharyngioma) are all plausible pituitary/parasellar lesions that can undergo hemorrhage.  "RCC" here most likely refers to Rathke cleft cyst, a common benign cystic lesion in the pituitary region, rather than renal cell carcinoma.

**Consider Surgical and Histopathological Findings:** Transsphenoidal microsurgery and the description of the surgical contents ("bloody, mucinous, and yellowish cystic contents") are highly suggestive of a Rathke cleft cyst. Rathke cleft cysts are typically cystic and can contain mucinous fluid and blood products, especially if hemorrhage has occurred.  The histopathology confirming "RCC with epithelial lining and evidence of acute hemorrhage" definitively diagnoses the lesion as a Rathke cleft cyst. The "epithelial lining" is characteristic of Rathke cleft cysts, which originate from remnants of Rathke's pouch.  "Acute hemorrhage" explains the patient's sudden onset of severe headache and vomiting, as hemorrhage into a Rathke cleft cyst can cause rapid cyst expansion and acute symptoms due to pressure on surrounding structures.

**Review Follow-up Information:** Postoperative resolution of the cyst on imaging confirms successful surgical removal. Postoperative hypothyroidism and transient secondary adrenal insufficiency are recognized complications of transsphenoidal surgery due to manipulation of the pituitary gland. These are manageable with hormone replacement and are expected to resolve or stabilize over time. The absence of recurrence at 3-month follow-up is a positive indicator of successful treatment.

**Synthesize all findings to reach the Final Diagnosis:**  Combining the clinical presentation of sudden severe headache and vomiting, imaging findings of a hemorrhagic pituitary lesion, surgical findings of cystic contents, and definitive histopathological diagnosis of "RCC with epithelial lining and evidence of acute hemorrhage," the final diagnosis is a Rathke cleft cyst with acute hemorrhage. The term "RCC" in the histopathology report is clearly an abbreviation for Rathke cleft cyst in this neurosurgical context.

gt_reasoning_eval.step: Step 1: Potential diagnoses based on imaging include pituitary adenoma, rathke's cleft cyst (rcc), and craniopharyngioma.
Step 2: Pituitary adenoma is ruled out based on histopathology and absence of adenomatous features.
Step 3: Rathke's cleft cyst (rcc) is considered and later confirmed by histopathology.
Step 4: Craniopharyngioma is excluded due to absence of characteristic calcifications and histological features.
Step 5: Key surgical and histopathological findings include cystic contents with serous and yellowish fluid, epithelial linings, and acute hemorrhage, confirming rathke's cleft cyst (rcc) hemorrhage.
Step 6: Preoperative mri differentiation of rathke's cleft cyst from other sellar lesions (adenomas or craniopharyngiomas) was challenging; confirmation required histological examination.
Step 7: Postoperative evaluation shows persistent hypothyroidism and transient adrenal insufficiency due to pituitary gland compression and post-surgical manipulation.
Step 8: Final diagnosis: rathke's cleft cyst with apoplexy (hemorrhage), which mimics pituitary apoplexy.
